section.title: The Twelve Spies
section.sectionRationale: At the very edge of the promised land, twelve leaders are sent to spy out Canaan, and their divided report, ten voices of fear against Caleb's lone voice of faith, sets up the great national crisis of chapter 14, making this one of the wilderness narratives the New Testament treats as an abiding warning against unbelief, 1 Corinthians 10:1-13, Hebrews 3-4.
section.thesis: Twelve leaders see the same good and fruitful land and the same formidable inhabitants, yet ten choose to measure God's promise by the size of the obstacle while Caleb alone measures the obstacle by the size of God's promise, showing that unbelief and faith can look at identical facts and reach opposite conclusions.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the twelve leaders named and sent to spy out Canaan; Moses' specific instructions for what to observe; the spies' forty-day journey and their report of a rich but formidably defended land; and Caleb's call to go up at once against the ten spies' report of fear.

units.label: The twelve leaders sent to spy out Canaan

verses.text: And the LORD said to Moses,
“Send out for yourself men to {{spy|spy}} out the land of Canaan, which I am giving to the Israelites. From each of their fathers' tribes send one man who is a leader among them.”
So at the consent of the LORD, Moses sent them out from the Wilderness of Paran. All the men were leaders of the Israelites,

and these were their names: 
From the tribe of Reuben, Shammua son of Zaccur;
from the tribe of Simeon, Shaphat son of Hori;
from the tribe of Judah, Caleb son of Jephunneh;
from the tribe of Issachar, Igal son of Joseph;
from the tribe of Ephraim, Hoshea son of Nun;
from the tribe of Benjamin, Palti son of Raphu;
from the tribe of Zebulun, Gaddiel son of Sodi;
from the tribe of Manasseh, a tribe of Joseph, Gaddi son of Susi;
from the tribe of Dan, Ammiel son of Gemalli;
from the tribe of Asher, Sethur son of Michael;
from the tribe of Naphtali, Nahbi son of Vophsi;
and from the tribe of Gad, Geuel son of Machi.
These were the names of the men Moses sent to spy out the land; and Moses gave to Hoshea son of Nun the name Joshua.
When Moses sent them to spy out the land of Canaan, he told them, “Go up through the Negev and into the hill country.
See what the land is like and whether its people are strong or weak, few or many.
Is the land where they live good or bad? Are the cities where they dwell open camps or fortifications?
Is the soil fertile or unproductive? Are there trees in it or not? Be courageous, and bring back some of the fruit of the land.” (It was the season for the first ripe grapes.)

units.label: The spies' journey and the report of the land

verses.text: So they went up and spied out the land from the Wilderness of Zin as far as Rehob, toward Lebo-hamath.
They went up through the Negev and came to Hebron, where Ahiman, Sheshai, and Talmai, the descendants of Anak, dwelled. It had been built seven years before Zoan in Egypt.
When they came to the Valley of Eshcol, they cut down a branch with a single cluster of grapes, which they carried on a pole between two men. They also took some pomegranates and figs.
Because of the cluster of grapes the Israelites cut there, that place was called the Valley of Eshcol.
After forty days the men returned from spying out the land,

and they went back to Moses, Aaron, and the whole congregation of Israel in the Wilderness of Paran at Kadesh. They brought back a report for the whole congregation and showed them the fruit of the land.
And they gave this account to Moses: “We went into the land to which you sent us, and indeed, it is flowing with milk and honey. Here is some of its fruit!
Nevertheless, the people living in the land are strong, and the cities are large and fortified. We even saw the descendants of Anak there.
The Amalekites live in the land of the Negev; the Hittites, Jebusites, and Amorites live in the hill country; and the Canaanites live by the sea and along the Jordan.”

units.label: Caleb's call to go up, and the ten spies' fear

verses.text: Then Caleb quieted the people before Moses and said, “We must go up and take possession of the land, for we can certainly conquer it!”
But the men who had gone up with him replied, “We cannot go up against the people, for they are stronger than we are!”
So they gave the Israelites a bad report about the land that they had spied out: “The land we explored devours its inhabitants, and all the people we saw there are great in stature.
We even saw the Nephilim there, the descendants of Anak that come from the Nephilim! We seemed like grasshoppers in our own sight, and we must have seemed the same to them!”

spy.gloss: To spy out, explore, or seek out. Describes the careful, deliberate scouting the twelve leaders undertook; both the fearful majority and Caleb's faithful minority saw the identical explored land, showing the decisive factor was not the evidence but the faith or fear interpreting it.

basic.context: At the border of Canaan, Moses sends twelve tribal leaders to spy out the land the LORD is giving Israel. After forty days, they return with real fruit as proof of the land's richness, but ten of the twelve report that the land's inhabitants are too strong to overcome, while Caleb alone insists Israel can certainly take the land because the LORD is with them.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing an actual reconnaissance mission and its divided report, and the New Testament treats the ensuing unbelief as a direct warning: 1 Corinthians 10:1-13 and Hebrews 3-4 use this very episode to warn believers against hardening their hearts through unbelief. The chapter itself is descriptive, showing exactly how fear distorts perception even of good and factual evidence.
basic.originalLanguages: The verb tur, to spy out or explore, describes a careful, deliberate scouting mission, not a casual glance; both the ten spies and Caleb saw the same tur, the same explored land, yet interpreted the identical facts through radically different lenses of fear and faith.
basic.typology: Caleb's minority report of faith, standing against the majority's fear despite identical evidence, anticipates the pattern of the faithful remnant throughout Scripture, and ultimately Christ, who alone perfectly trusted the Father's promises against every appearance of overwhelming opposition, even the cross itself.
basic.christ: Where the ten spies concluded we cannot despite God's explicit promise and presence, Christ is the ultimate yes to every promise of God, 2 Corinthians 1:20, trusting His Father's word completely even facing an obstacle infinitely greater than fortified cities, the cross itself, and emerging victorious exactly as promised.
basic.god: God has already promised this good land to Israel and demonstrated His power decisively in the exodus and at Sinai, yet the spies' report reveals how easily His people can let visible obstacles eclipse His spoken word. God's patience with this moment of fear-filled reporting, before the actual rebellion breaks out in chapter 14, shows Him giving space for faith to answer fear before judgment falls.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 10:1-13 warns believers directly from Israel's wilderness failures, including this episode. Hebrews 3:16-19 asks who provoked God, identifying it as those who came out of Egypt. 2 Corinthians 1:20 declares that every one of God's promises is yes in Christ. Deuteronomy 1:19-33 retells this episode from Moses' own later perspective.
basic.oneStory: Israel stands at the very border of the promised land with God's word and power already proven, yet ten leaders let fear override the promise while one, Caleb, trusts God's word against every visible obstacle. That contrast between fear and faith runs through the whole Bible and finds its resolution in Christ, in whom every promise of God is answered with a decisive yes.

context.literary: This chapter sets up the crisis of chapter 14, forming with it a single narrative unit that becomes, alongside Korah's rebellion, one of the two defining moments of unbelief that seal the fate of the exodus generation.
context.historical: The forty days of spying directly determine the forty years of wandering announced in chapter 14, a deliberate correspondence, a year for each day, that ties this episode's length to the judgment that follows.
context.canonical: This episode becomes a central Old Testament example of unbelief cited repeatedly in the New Testament, most extensively in Hebrews 3 and 4, where the writer urges believers not to harden their hearts as this generation did at the very edge of God's promised rest.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show precisely how the same objective facts, a good land defended by strong inhabitants, can be interpreted through either fear or faith, and to highlight Caleb's exceptional trust as the standard against which the majority's failure is measured.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter describes a specific historical reconnaissance and its divided report; it is not merely a template for how to conduct evaluations, but the New Testament explicitly reads its outcome as a prescriptive warning against unbelief for every generation of God's people, 1 Corinthians 10:11.

debates.question: Were the ten spies wrong in their factual observations about the land and its inhabitants?

views.label: Their facts were largely accurate, but their conclusion was faithless
views.case: The land was indeed inhabited by strong peoples in fortified cities, and even Caleb does not dispute this; the sin lay not in observing real obstacles but in concluding we cannot despite God's explicit promise and presence.

views.label: Their report included deliberate exaggeration
views.case: Phrases like the land devours its inhabitants and comparing themselves to grasshoppers, verses 32 to 33, go beyond simple reporting into fear-driven exaggeration meant to dissuade the people from advancing.

debates.bearing: Either way, the chapter's central lesson holds: facts alone do not determine faith or unbelief; the same evidence, honestly or exaggeratedly reported, becomes an occasion for fear or a platform for trusting God's promise, depending on the heart interpreting it.

study.originalLanguages: The verb tur, to spy out or explore, describes the careful, deliberate scouting these twelve men undertook. Both the majority report and Caleb's minority report describe the identical explored territory, showing that the deciding factor was not the information itself but the faith or fear interpreting it.

typology.type: Caleb's lone voice of faith against the ten spies' majority fear
typology.fulfillment: The faithful remnant theme running through Scripture, and ultimately Christ, who alone perfectly trusted the Father against every appearance of overwhelming opposition.

typology.type: The good land seen but not yet possessed
typology.fulfillment: The full inheritance promised to believers in Christ, already guaranteed yet not fully possessed until the consummation, Ephesians 1:13-14.

study.christ: Where the ten spies concluded we cannot despite God's explicit promise and presence, Christ is the ultimate yes to every promise of God, 2 Corinthians 1:20, trusting His Father's word completely even facing an obstacle infinitely greater than fortified cities, the cross itself, and emerging victorious exactly as promised.
study.god: God has already promised this good land to Israel and demonstrated His power decisively in the exodus and at Sinai, yet the spies' report reveals how easily His people can let visible obstacles eclipse His spoken word. God's patience with this moment of fear-filled reporting, before the actual rebellion breaks out in chapter 14, shows Him giving space for faith to answer fear before judgment falls.

crossReferences.note: Warns believers directly from Israel's wilderness failures, including this episode of unbelief at the border of Canaan.
Asks who provoked God, identifying it as those who came out of Egypt, referencing this very generation's unbelief.
Declares that every one of God's promises is yes in Christ, the answer to the spies' faithless no.
Retells this episode from Moses' own later perspective, reinforcing its significance for Israel's memory.

study.oneStory: Israel stands at the very border of the promised land with God's word and power already proven, yet ten leaders let fear override the promise while one, Caleb, trusts God's word against every visible obstacle. That contrast between fear and faith runs through the whole Bible and finds its resolution in Christ, in whom every promise of God is answered with a decisive yes.

sermon.title: Fear, Faith and Courage
sermon.why: Preached on Numbers 13-14, Keller explores how biblical courage is inseparable from faith, showing that the ultimate fear behind Israel's unbelief was a mistrust in God, and that true security is found only in resting completely on Him.

quiz.q: What did Moses instruct the twelve spies to observe about the land, besides its people?

options.text: Whether the land was good or bad, whether the soil was fertile, whether there were trees, and whether the cities were fortified.
options.note: Verses 18 to 20 list these specific instructions.

options.text: Only the exact population count of every city.
options.note: Moses' instructions are broader than a mere census, covering soil, produce, and fortifications as well.

options.text: Only whether gold and silver could be found there.
options.note: This is not part of Moses' instructions in verses 18 to 20.

options.text: No specific instructions are given.
options.note: Verses 18 to 20 give detailed instructions.

quiz.q: What evidence did the spies bring back to demonstrate the land's fruitfulness?

options.text: A single cluster of grapes so large it was carried on a pole between two men, along with pomegranates and figs.
options.note: Verses 23 and 26 describe this evidence exactly.

options.text: Detailed maps of every city's walls.
options.note: No such maps are described in the text.

options.text: Written testimonies from local residents.
options.note: This is not part of the report described in the chapter.

options.text: No physical evidence is mentioned.
options.note: Verses 23 and 26 describe the physical evidence brought back.

quiz.q: What was Caleb's response to the fearful report of the other ten spies?

options.text: He quieted the people and insisted they should go up and take possession of the land because they could certainly conquer it.
options.note: Verse 30 records his exact response.

options.text: He agreed entirely with the ten spies' assessment.
options.note: This contradicts the text; Caleb directly opposes their conclusion in verse 30.

options.text: He remained silent and offered no opinion.
options.note: This contradicts his direct, recorded speech in verse 30.

options.text: Caleb is not mentioned in this chapter.
options.note: Caleb is named and speaks directly in verse 30.

quiz.q: How did the ten spies describe themselves in comparison to the land's inhabitants?

options.text: Like grasshoppers in their own sight, and, they assumed, in the eyes of the inhabitants as well.
options.note: Verse 33 records this exact self-description.

options.text: As mighty warriors equal to any nation.
options.note: This contradicts their fearful self-description in verse 33.

options.text: As wealthier and better equipped than the Canaanites.
options.note: This is not part of their described report.

options.text: No such comparison is made in the text.
options.note: Verse 33 makes this comparison directly.

quiz.q: What did the spies report about the inhabitants of the land, alongside its fruitfulness?

options.text: That they were strong, the cities were large and fortified, and the descendants of Anak were seen there.
options.note: Verses 28 to 29 and 32 to 33 describe this report exactly.

options.text: That the land was completely uninhabited.
options.note: This contradicts the extensive description of its inhabitants throughout the report.

options.text: That the inhabitants had already fled at Israel's approach.
options.note: This contradicts the report of a strong, present population, verses 28 to 29.

options.text: No report about the inhabitants is given.
options.note: Verses 28 to 29 and 32 to 33 describe the inhabitants in detail.

questions: Ten spies and Caleb saw the exact same land, the exact same obstacles, yet reached completely opposite conclusions. What situation in your life are you currently viewing through fear rather than through the lens of God's proven faithfulness?

The ten spies' report contained real facts but was ultimately faithless in its conclusion, we cannot. How do you distinguish between honestly acknowledging a difficulty and using that difficulty as an excuse for unbelief?

Caleb stood almost entirely alone against the majority's fear, trusting God's promise over the crowd's consensus. Where do you need the courage to hold a minority position of faith, even when most around you are moving toward fear or compromise?

The spies described themselves as grasshoppers compared to the land's inhabitants, a self-image that shaped their entire outlook. How does your view of yourself, rather than your view of God, sometimes determine whether you step forward in faith or shrink back in fear?

This whole episode is remembered in the New Testament as a direct warning against hardening your heart in unbelief. What promise of God are you currently at risk of doubting because the obstacle in front of you looks too large?

translationNotes.note: Moses gave to Hoshea son of Nun the name Joshua marks a significant renaming, from Hoshea, meaning salvation, to Joshua, meaning the LORD saves, foreshadowing his future role leading Israel into the land.
It had been built seven years before Zoan in Egypt is an editorial aside comparing Hebron's antiquity to a well-known Egyptian city, likely added for readers familiar with Egyptian chronology.

The Nephilim, mentioned earlier in Genesis 6:4, are invoked here to heighten the sense of overwhelming, almost legendary opposition; whether this is an accurate identification or fear-driven exaggeration is debated among interpreters.
